assessor_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT — real_world_framing_005

Sensitivity 0.94 for pulmonary nodules ≥6mm is a strong result comparable to fellowship-trained radiologist benchmarks. The after-hours coverage problem is real — limited on-call coverage and documented patient safety events from delayed nodule identification are genuine clinical needs. However, the proposed deployment plan has three serious problems that make it inappropriate as proposed.

First, the hardware distribution shift: the hospital replaced both primary CT scanners in late 2023 with new-generation hardware that uses different slice thickness defaults and a different reconstruction algorithm. The model's validation data was entirely from pre-upgrade hardware. In radiology deep learning, CT scanner hardware changes — particularly reconstruction algorithm and slice thickness — are known to materially affect model performance. The reported sensitivity of 0.94 was achieved on a different input distribution than the prospective deployment will use. The hospital has no evidence of model performance on new-hardware images.

Second, the liability exposure: reporting model findings directly to ordering physicians without radiologist sign-off is outside standard of care for radiological interpretation in the United States. The hospital's malpractice insurer has not been consulted. Any adverse patient outcome resulting from a model-only report creates an uninsured malpractice claim. This is not a marginal risk — it is the predictable consequence of operating outside the coverage scope.

Third, the archive selection bias: PACS archives over-represent cases with previously-identified significant findings. The retrospective sensitivity of 0.94 was measured in a higher-prevalence case mix than a prospective deployment would encounter. Sensitivity is likely overestimated for the real-world case mix.

The correct architecture is human-in-the-loop: model-assisted review where the on-call radiologist receives model findings as a second reader, with radiologist sign-off required before any report is released to the ordering physician. This addresses the after-hours coverage problem without the patient safety and liability risks of autonomous reporting, and can be implemented after prospective hardware validation.

Verdict: Deployment as proposed is inappropriate.
